title: Design: rework settings panel to be contextual to camera mode (nav vs observe)
description:
The right-side settings panel today shows a single static control set regardless of which mode the user is in. Navigate and observe expose different affordances and want different things at the user's fingertips, so the panel ends up cluttered for both. This is a usability/UX issue: the user wants ready access to mode-relevant controls without scanning the whole panel.

Deliverable: a written design spec (in docs/ or attached to this issue) covering, at minimum:
- For each mode, which controls are primary vs secondary vs hidden.
- Layout: are mode-specific sections inserted/removed, or shown via tab/segmented control, or via collapse-by-default rules?
- Behaviour on mode-switch — animation, scroll position, collapse memory.
- Are there observe-mode controls that don't yet exist and should be added (e.g. FOV preset buttons, label-density slider) — flag them for separate feature issues, do not add scope here.
- Reverse-sync implications: today the panel reads back from Stellata.getFilter()/state; the design must keep that contract intact.

Critical files to read before designing:
- src/client/controls.ts — every existing widget + reverse-sync.
- src/client/panel-layout.ts — top-level + per-group collapse behaviour the redesign must respect or replace.
- src/client/mode-toggle.ts — the navigate↔observe pill that triggers the mode change the panel needs to react to.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — affordances that exist only in observe (drag, FOV wheel) and should inform what observe surfaces in the panel.
- docs/ui-and-controls.md — documents existing layout containers, magnitude presets/override flags, CSS gotchas ([hidden] specificity, backdrop-filter stacking contexts).

Out of scope: implementation. Once the design is approved, file an implementation issue and close this one.

title: iOS Safari: app doesn't fill the viewport — URL bar steals space
description:
On iOS Safari, the canvas + UI don't expand into the full visible area: the bottom URL bar / browser chrome compresses the available height and the app feels "letterboxed" rather than native. Modern web apps render full-bleed using the dynamic-viewport units, the viewport-fit=cover meta directive, and safe-area-inset-* env variables — Stellata likely uses static 100vh (or equivalent) and a default viewport meta, so it loses the URL-bar zone.

Suspected fix surface (confirm during implementation):
- src/client/index.html — viewport meta needs viewport-fit=cover; consider apple-mobile-web-app-capable metadata for an even more native feel when added to the home screen.
- src/client/styles.css — every 100vh / vh height should migrate to 100dvh / dvh (dynamic viewport units, supported in iOS 15.4+). Add padding: env(safe-area-inset-*) where the panel/topbar/overlays touch the screen edge so they don't sit under the notch or home indicator.
- The Three.js canvas resize path in src/client/main.ts / src/client/stellata.ts — verify the renderer picks up the new height correctly when the URL bar shows/hides during scroll (resize event must fire and update the camera aspect).

Verify before close:
- Open the deployed app on a real iPhone in Safari. The canvas reaches the bottom of the visible area; the URL bar overlays without leaving a gap.
- Rotate to landscape; canvas still fills the viewport.
- "Add to home screen" launches in something close to standalone chrome (or document a follow-up if PWA work is required).
- No regressions on desktop Chrome/Safari/Firefox or Android Chrome.
- npm run typecheck and npm test pass.

Out of scope: full PWA install / service-worker / offline support (that's a separate epic if we ever go there). Mobile gestures and panel layout (those land under the audit-driven subtasks).

title: Audit mobile UX and produce subtask breakdown under the mobile epic
description:
Investigative subtask whose deliverable is the rest of the mobile epic's subtask list. Walk through every documented interaction on a real iPhone and a real Android device (or emulators if hardware is unavailable) and document what's broken, missing, or degraded.

Coverage checklist (with the relevant source for each):
- Camera controls — src/client/stellata.ts (TrackballControls), src/client/observe-controls.ts (look-around + FOV).
- Settings panel — src/client/controls.ts, src/client/panel-layout.ts, src/client/styles.css.
- Modals — src/client/info-modal.ts, brand-modal.ts, help-modal.ts, the goModal/conModal plumbing in keyboard-shortcuts.ts, and constellation-typeahead.ts.
- Search inputs — src/client/search.ts, src/client/constellation-typeahead.ts.
- Top-bar & affordances — src/client/warp-button.ts, mode-toggle.ts, brand-box markup in src/client/index.html.
- Overlays — every *-overlay.ts and disc-mask.ts, plus scale-bar.ts.
- Render perf baseline — src/client/main.ts + the perf hooks noted in docs/performance.md (debug.perf()); record FPS numbers in the issue notes.

Deliverable: file the follow-up subtasks under the parent epic, with rough priorities. Then close this issue.

Out of scope: actually fixing anything. This issue is investigation only.

title: Mobile experience: make Stellata workable on phones / small touch devices
description:
Stellata today is built and tuned for desktop — TrackballControls with mouse + wheel, a fixed right-side settings panel, modals sized for desktop viewports, and a render budget tuned for discrete GPUs. The result on phones is degraded and in places unusable: gestures don't map cleanly (no pinch-to-zoom, two-finger roll is desktop-Safari-only by design), the panel covers the canvas, modals overflow, and performance has not been characterised on mobile chips.

Goal: make Stellata a deliberate, usable mobile experience without compromising the desktop product. Concrete subtasks are deferred to the audit issue below — touch gestures, responsive panel layout, modal sizing, mobile-perf characterisation, and any catalog/dust downscaling decisions are the likely categories but should be confirmed by the audit before being broken out.

Out of scope (for now): a separate mobile build, native app packaging, replacing TrackballControls wholesale on desktop.

Critical files (for orientation when subtasks are scoped):
- src/client/controls.ts — right-side settings panel layout/widgets.
- src/client/stellata.ts — TrackballControls wiring + camera state machine.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — drag/yaw/pitch + wheel FOV (desktop-tuned).
- src/client/styles.css, src/client/index.html — viewport meta, layout containers.
- docs/camera-modes.md, docs/ui-and-controls.md — current desktop assumptions (two-finger roll = Safari-only, panel reverse-sync, etc.).

This epic closes only when its subtasks are all closed.

title: Further compress the view URL parameter
description:
The `?v=` blob (schema v2, in `src/client/url-state.ts`) pays a fixed 12-byte cost per vec3 (`cam`, `tgt`, `up`) whenever **any single component** diverges from its default. A camera at `[0, 0, 3.7]` — the common case for a near-Sol orbit on the z-axis — burns 8 bytes encoding two zero-valued floats.

Worked example: `https://stellata.xyz/?v=AgEAAAAAAAAAAAAAPtRsQA` decodes to:

- byte 0: `0x02` — version 2
- bytes 1–3: `0x000001` — presence mask, only bit 0 (`cam`) set
- bytes 4–15: cam float32 LE × 3 = `[0.0, 0.0, ~3.7004]`

→ 16 bytes, 22 base64url chars. 8 of those bytes (≈11 chars) are zero floats.

Two compression opportunities:

1. **Per-component vec3 encoding.** Only emit floats for components that diverge from the per-component default. The defaults already vary per vec3 (`cam=[0,0,30]` navigate / `[0,0,0]` observe; `tgt=[0,0,0]`; `up=[0,1,0]`), so a per-component compare is the natural extension of the existing whole-vec3 default check at `url-state.ts:614-623`.

2. **Varint presence mask.** The 24-bit mask is often near-zero in its high bytes (the example has `0x00 0x00` for bytes 2–3). LEB128 (7 bits payload + continuation bit) collapses single-bit masks to 1 byte instead of 3.

The exponent-notation idea from the original sketch (encode `1.24` in fewer bytes than full float32) is harder to land cleanly without losing round-trip exactness. float32 is already a binary-exponent format; shrinking to float16 trades ~3 sig-figs of precision and breaks at galactic distances. Keeping it as out-of-scope for the first pass — possibly worth revisiting just for the `up` vector, which is always normalised and ≤1.0 per component.

Headline impact for the example URL: 22 chars → ~12 chars (≈45% shorter), with no precision loss.
design:
# Schema v3 (in `src/client/url-state.ts`)

Bump `SCHEMA_VERSION` from 2 to 3. Keep `FIELDS_V1`/`decodeV1` and `FIELDS_V2`/`decodeV2` frozen verbatim — same pattern already in use. Add `FIELDS_V3` and `decodeV3`. The auto-upgrade rewrite at `applyFromUrl:778` already triggers when `decoded.version !== SCHEMA_VERSION`, so legacy URLs in the address bar will be silently rewritten to v3 on next load.

## Change 1 — per-component vec3 encoding (primary win)

Replace `vec3Field` for v3 with a variant that emits a 1-byte sub-mask prefix:

```
[1 byte: lower 3 bits = which components diverge from default]
[for each set bit: 4-byte f32 LE]
```

Per-vec3 byte cost (vs. v2's flat 12 bytes whenever the field is present):

| diverging components | v3 bytes  | v2 bytes | Δ  |
|----------------------|-----------|----------|-----|
| 0 (field omitted)    | 0         | 0        | 0   |
| 1                    | 1 + 4 = 5 | 12       | −7  |
| 2                    | 1 + 8 = 9 | 12       | −3  |
| 3                    | 1 + 12 = 13 | 12     | +1  |

`isPresent` returns true iff any component diverges from the per-component default — same trigger as today, just per-component. Components with bit clear fill in from the per-component default at decode time.

### Wrinkle: `cam` default depends on mode

The encoder already handles this at `currentStateOf:614` (`camDefault = mode === 'observe' ? [0,0,0] : DEFAULT_CAM`). The decoder doesn't know mode at the point it decodes `cam`, because mode comes from the `flags` field (bit 13). Two options:

- **(a) Two-pass decode.** Decode all fields, then post-fix `cam` defaults using the now-known mode. Cheap (one extra branch after the field loop) but couples decode order.
- **(b) Always treat cam default as `[0,0,30]` on the wire.** Simpler, but sacrifices the savings on observe-mode shares where `cam.z=0`.

**Recommend (a).** The post-decode fix is a single `if (mask & cam_bit && view.cam) { ... }` check after the loop, reading `view.mode` from the already-decoded `flags`. No structural change to the field-loop machinery.

### Defaults table

Hoist a per-key default table at module scope so encoder and decoder share one source of truth:

```ts
const VEC3_DEFAULTS_NAVIGATE: Record<'cam'|'tgt'|'up', readonly [number,number,number]> = {
  cam: [0, 0, 30], tgt: [0, 0, 0], up: [0, 1, 0],
};
const VEC3_DEFAULTS_OBSERVE_CAM: readonly [number, number, number] = [0, 0, 0];
```

## Change 2 — varint presence mask (secondary win, separate commit)

Replace the fixed 3-byte mask with LEB128:

- 7 bits payload + 1 continuation bit per byte
- Mask `0x000001` → 1 byte (saves 2)
- Mask `0x000800` → 2 bytes (saves 1)
- Mask `0x080001` → 3 bytes (no change)

`encodeBlob` writes the varint after the version byte; `decodeV3` reads the varint and tracks the resulting offset before stepping through fields. No changes to per-field encode/decode contracts.

Land as a follow-up commit on top of Change 1 so the v3 round-trip suite already exists when the mask format moves.

## Test plan

`src/client/url-state.test.ts` already has byte-length assertions and round-trip suites. Extend with:

- v3 round-trip block — copy the pattern from the existing vec3 / scalar / focus / POI suites, but assert tighter byte budgets (e.g. `cam=[0,0,3.7]` blob ≤ 9 bytes, ≤ 12 base64url chars).
- v2-decode regression block — clone the v1-decode regression at `url-state.test.ts:421+`. Encode a known view via the v2 path (need a tiny test helper that runs `FIELDS_V2`-flavoured encode), feed to `decodeBlob`, assert the same `view` falls out and `version === 2`.
- Component-default fill tests — `roundtrip({ cam: [0, 0, 3.7] })` returns `cam: [0, 0, 3.7]` (decoder fills the omitted x/y from default). `roundtrip({ tgt: [0, 0, 0] })` omits the field entirely. Mode-aware cam default: encode with `mode: 'observe'` and `cam: [0, 0, 0]` → field omitted; with navigate and `cam: [0, 0, 0]` → cam present (because navigate default is `[0, 0, 30]`).
- Update the empty-blob length assertion at `url-state.test.ts:18` if Change 2 lands (varint mask shrinks empty to 2 bytes / 3 chars).

## Out of scope

- float16 / varint-mantissa float encoding for cam/tgt. Addressed in description — defer.
- float16 for `up` only (always normalised, ≤1.0). Tempting; revisit once Change 1 is landed and we have measurements.
- Compressing other fields (`spect` is a 9-bit mask, can't shrink; `dmin`/`dmax` u16 already maps to slider step count, u8 would lose granularity).

## Files touched

- `src/client/url-state.ts` — primary
- `src/client/url-state.test.ts` — regression coverage
acceptance_criteria:
- Existing v1 and v2 URLs continue to decode unchanged (regression suite enforces this).
- `?v=` blob for `cam = [0, 0, 3.7]` with no other state drops from 22 base64url chars to ≤14 chars (and ≤12 chars after Change 2).
- Default state still produces no `?v=` query param at all.
- Fully-populated vec3s (every component non-default) grow by at most 1 byte per vec3 — accepted trade.
- Round-trip exactness preserved for every v3 field (no precision loss vs. v2 within the schema's already-quantised scalar grid).
- Vitest suite extended with v3 round-trips and a v2-decode regression block matching the existing v1-regression coverage at `url-state.test.ts:421+`.
- Address bar auto-rewrites legacy URLs to v3 on load (existing `applyFromUrl` upgrade pattern, no new code needed).
- Mode-dependent `cam` default (`[0,0,30]` navigate / `[0,0,0]` observe) handled correctly on encode and decode; covered by tests.

title: /docs/* markdown content for all 7 pages
description:
Drafts user-facing markdown for every /docs/* page. Depends on stellata-8c1.1 (pipeline must exist first).

CRITICAL: Drafted content must be sourced from CURRENT code/data, not from memory or assumption. There is a project memory entry about famous stars that don't appear in the catalog (μ Cep et al). The limitations.md page deliberately mirrors that.

Pages (write all 7 in this single issue):
- content/docs/index.md — TOC + intro
- content/docs/navigate-mode.md — TrackballControls, click states, focus ring, distance vector, panel filters, magnitude presets, warp button. Source: docs/architecture.md, docs/camera-modes.md, src/client/controls.ts, warp-button.ts.
- content/docs/observe-mode.md — drag yaw/pitch, wheel FOV, two-finger roll (Safari-only desktop), pinned POIs. Source: docs/camera-modes.md, src/client/observe-controls.ts, mode-toggle.ts.
- content/docs/keyboard-shortcuts.md — mirror of help-modal content + the shortcuts dispatched in keyboard-shortcuts.ts. Source: src/client/help-modal.ts, keyboard-shortcuts.ts.
- content/docs/the-data.md — AT-HYG v3.3, GCVS, Hipparcos CCDM, Stellarium constellation lines, Edenhofer dust, Zucker molecular clouds (note: shelved). Link to SCIENCE.md for full citations. Source: SCIENCE.md, docs/build-and-data.md.
- content/docs/the-physics.md — magnitudes & physical radius, spectral class → temperature → colour, super-Gaussian intensity profile, variable pulsation (R ∝ √L assumption), dust extinction, Milky Way volumetric model, magnitude consistency conversion. Source: docs/rendering.md, docs/milky-way.md, SCIENCE.md.
- content/docs/limitations.md — proper motion not animated, missing stars (μ Cep et al), all-sky dust raymarch deferred, irregular variables skipped, temperature swing in variables not modelled, rectangular block artifact past mag ~9, molecular clouds shelved for v1. Source: CLAUDE.md 'Things deliberately kept out' + 'Temporarily shelved' sections, project memory.

Each markdown file gets frontmatter: title, description (used for <title>, meta description, OG tags via the docs template).

Public docs should be friendlier and shorter than internal docs/* — not a 1:1 copy. Cite SCIENCE.md and the GitHub repo for deep-dive readers.

Verify before close:
- /docs index TOC renders, sidebar shows all 7 pages
- Each /docs/<page> renders end-to-end, anchor links work, sidebar highlights current page
- No factual claims contradicted by current src/ or data/ — spot-check the limitations page against project memory and CLAUDE.md
- Lighthouse SEO score on /docs ≥ 95

title: Build pipeline + multi-entry Vite + skeleton pages
description:
Lays the plumbing for the /about and /docs work. Goal: by the end of this issue, /about and /docs/index render as empty/skeleton pages and the build pipeline regenerates docs HTML on .md change in dev.

Scope:
- Add markdown-it + @types/markdown-it + markdown-it-anchor + gray-matter as devDependencies (use Node 22 fs.globSync rather than the glob package; verify node --version ≥ 22 first).
- Create scripts/build-docs.ts — reads content/docs/*.md, applies gray-matter frontmatter, renders body via markdown-it (+ anchor plugin), wraps with src/client/_docs-template.html, writes to src/client/docs/<slug>.html. Also writes public/sitemap.xml from the same file list.
- Create scripts/vite-plugin-docs.ts — buildStart runs build-docs.ts once; configureServer chokidar-watches content/docs/**/*.md and the template, regenerates on change, sends Vite HMR full-reload signal.
- Update vite.config.ts to use a multi-entry rollupOptions.input via fs.globSync('src/client/**/*.html', { ignore: '**/_*.html' }) and register vite-plugin-docs.
- Create src/client/_docs-template.html with placeholders ({{TITLE}}, {{DESCRIPTION}}, {{CONTENT}}, {{SIDEBAR}}, {{CANONICAL}}).
- Create src/client/about.html as a minimal skeleton (title + h1 only — content lands in the about issue).
- Create src/client/site.css (minimal; full styling lands in the about issue).
- Create src/client/site/site.ts (empty stub for mobile nav toggle / smooth-scroll).
- Create content/docs/index.md as a skeleton (frontmatter + a heading; full content lands in docs-content issue).
- Add public/robots.txt (allow all + sitemap link) and public/favicon.svg (small SVG mark).
- Update package.json: add 'build:docs': 'tsx scripts/build-docs.ts' and wire it into 'build' (after build:dust-sync, before build:client) and 'dev' (run once before vite, plugin handles incremental).
- Update .gitignore: add src/client/docs/ (generated).

Verify before close:
- npm run typecheck passes
- npm test still passes (244 tests, no behavioural change to app code)
- npm run build:docs alone produces src/client/docs/index.html
- npm run build produces dist/about.html and dist/docs/index.html with hashed asset refs
- npm run dev: editing content/docs/index.md triggers a Vite reload
- curl-test deployed preview that /docs/index serves at both /docs/index and /docs/index/

This issue blocks: about, docs-content, seo-nav.

The app stays at / so direct traffic still lands in the WebGL explorer.

Full design lives in:

/Users/alexm/.claude/plans/resume-planning-of-users-alexm-claude-pl-bright-origami.md (resumed plan with beads breakdown)

title: Phase 7: ATHYG reduced_m12 catalog upgrade
description:
**Goal:** populate the resolved-discrete star layer at intermediate travel distances (50 pc – ~kpc from Sol), where `classic_ids` runs out of entries because faint distant stars rarely have classical designations.

Complements Phase 5. Phase 5 fills the diffuse glow at distance; this phase fills the point sources at intermediate range. Together they make travel away from Sol feel populated at every scale.

**Data source:** ATHYG v3.3 `reduced_m12` subset — apparent magnitude ≤ 12, ~2M stars. Same source repository as the current `classic_ids`: https://codeberg.org/astronexus/athyg

**What changes:**
- `data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv` → replace with `data/athyg_33_reduced_m12.csv` (~400 MB CSV, LFS).
- `scripts/build-catalog.ts` reads the bigger CSV. Same field mapping; same 40-byte stride; same sort-by-absmag pipeline.
- Output `public/catalog.bin` grows from ~12 MB to ~80 MB (~20 MB gzipped).
- `search-index.json` content unchanged — only stars with classical identifiers go in the search index. The added ~1.7M nameless stars contribute to rendering only.
- GCVS variability cross-match picks up significantly more variables (more HIP/HD entries are present in the bigger catalog).
- Geometric binary inference runs over more stars; expect substantially more pairs found, but the `BINARY_MAX_SEP_PC = 0.005 pc` threshold keeps the count visually relevant.

**Watch out for:**
- Initial catalog download grows from ~3 MB gzipped to ~20 MB gzipped. Significant for first-load on slow connections. Worth measuring whether progressive load (render available chunks while the rest streams) is needed.
- 2M instanced quads at 60 fps needs profiling on modest hardware (integrated GPUs, mobile). The two-pass split already handles overdraw efficiently, but instance-attribute memory grows linearly. If there's a perf cliff, fall back to `reduced_m10` (~350k stars) as a smaller cut.
- `classic_ids` ⊂ `reduced_m12` in practice (stars with classical IDs are almost all brighter than mag 12 from Earth), but verify a few iconic targets (Proxima, Barnard's Star, Sol-itself) survive the switch.
- Constellation index assignment per star uses the AT-HYG `con` column unchanged — works for the bigger catalog out of the box.

**Estimate:** half-day for the build pipeline change + verification runs. Most of the time goes to catalog regeneration and perf testing.

title: Phase 6: Realism indicator + per-star dust slab fallback
description:
**Goal:** be honest about where the high-fidelity dust data ends without restricting where the user can travel. Free travel + clear information.

Two parts:

**Part 1 — slab fallback for per-star extinction.** Today `star.vert.glsl` raymarches camera→star through the Edenhofer voxel volume only. Lines of sight that exit the AABB get zero attenuation beyond — wrong direction at the edge. Borrow the analytical dust profile already shipped in `milkyway.frag.glsl` (`analyticalDustDensity`, `exp(-(R-R₀)/L) × exp(-|z|/H)` with h ≈ 125 pc, Galactic frame) and integrate it for the segment outside the voxel AABB. Per-star extinction then has a sensible value everywhere.

The function lives in the milky way shader today; lift it into a shared shader include or duplicate the constants when porting it into `star.vert.glsl`.

**Part 2 — high-fidelity-volume status indicator.** A small chip on the bottom row of the UI (next to the scale bar, in `.ui-bottom`) that toggles between:
- "high-fidelity dust volume" (camera world-position inside the Edenhofer AABB)
- "outside dust volume" (outside)

Optional toggle in the panel: "Show dust volume boundary" — a faint translucent sphere mesh of radius ~1.25 kpc at world origin. Off by default (subtle by design). When on, gives a 3D sense of where the high-fidelity data ends.

**Files:**
- Touch: `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` (call the shared slab function beyond AABB), `stellata.ts` (chip element + boundary mesh), `index.html` (chip markup), `styles.css` (chip styling), `controls.ts` (boundary toggle), `url-state.ts` (boundary toggle).

**Watch out for:**
- The chip should be unobtrusive — same visual weight as the scale bar, not a banner. If users want detail they'll toggle the boundary sphere.
- Slab evaluation in Galactic frame requires the same ICRS→Galactic rotation as Phase 5. Keep it as one shared shader function so both phases use identical maths.

**Estimate:** 3–5 hours, mostly UI polish.

title: Phase 4b: Radcliffe Wave spine
description:
**Goal:** glowing polyline showing the ~3 kpc oscillating gas wave that passes near the Sun. Famous nearby structure, narratively striking, very small data.

**Data sources:**
- **Alves et al. 2020** (Nature, original discovery).
- **Konietzka et al. 2024** — oscillation phase data.
- Catherine Zucker's GitHub / interactive gallery again is the practical entry point for the spine point list (~100 points).

**Implementation sketch:**
- Read ~100 (x, y, z) spine points from source. Tiny — kilobytes.
- Render as a `Line2` (Three.js fat line helper) with emissive shader, optionally colour-coded by oscillation phase from Konietzka 2024.
- Label "Radcliffe Wave" at midpoint of the spine.
- UI toggle.
- Bonus: when toggled on, highlight catalogued molecular clouds that are part of the wave (cross-ref against Phase 3a's cloud catalog).

**Watch out for:**
- Coordinate frame conversion (Galactic → ICRS).
- The wave extends across ~3 kpc, well past the 1.25 kpc Edenhofer volume; the spine itself is fine to render outside the dust grid, it just won't have associated dust at its extreme ends.

**Estimate:** 2–4 hours.

title: Phase 4a: Local Bubble shell mesh
description:
**Goal:** translucent surface mesh of the Local Bubble (low-density ISM cavity surrounding the Sun, ~200 pc radius). Subtle by default, toggleable for emphasis. Gives the user immediate context that the Sun sits *inside* a bubble.

**Data sources:**
- **O'Neill, Zucker, Goodman & Edenhofer 2024** ("The Local Bubble Is a Local Chimney", ApJ 973, 136 / arXiv:2407.18238). The paper should have a data availability section; if not, check Catherine Zucker's GitHub: https://github.com/catherinezucker
- **Zucker et al. 2022** (Nature, "Star formation near the Sun is driven by the expansion of the Local Bubble") — earlier 3D mesh. Also linked from her interactive 3D gallery at https://catherinezucker.github.io/

**Implementation sketch:**
- Source format likely OBJ/PLY/HDF5 mesh. Convert at preprocessor time to a tiny binary (vertex positions + face indices). Few thousand vertices typically.
- Three.js: `THREE.Mesh` with `THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ transparent: true, opacity: 0.05, side: DoubleSide, blending: AdditiveBlending })`.
- Subtle inner/outer gradient via vertex colours (front-facing vs back-facing) so users can perceive 3D shape.
- Label "Local Bubble" at the centroid, similar to constellation labels.
- UI toggle.

**Watch out for:**
- Coordinate frame — likely Galactic; rotate to ICRS on ingest.
- Mesh might be in different units (kpc vs pc); verify scale.
- Front-vs-back face ordering matters for the gradient effect.

**Estimate:** 3–5 hours.

title: Phase 3b: Nebulae catalogs (PN, RNe, H II, SNR)
description:
**Goal:** classical named nebulae (Orion Nebula, Ring Nebula, Helix, Veil, Horsehead, etc.) as discrete blob objects with type-based colour coding and search-bar entries.

**Data sources** (filter to within ~3 kpc to match the dust volume):
- **Strasbourg-ESO Catalogue of Galactic Planetary Nebulae** (Acker et al. 1992, updated): VizieR `V/84` or HEASARC `PLNEBULAE`. ~1,500 PNe with up to four independent distance estimates each.
- **Merged Catalog of Reflection Nebulae** (Magakian 2003): VizieR `J/A+A/399/141` or HEASARC `REFNEBULAE`. 913 reflection nebulae; cross-match the illuminating star to ATHYG via HD/HIP for distance.
- **Sharpless H II catalog**: VizieR `VII/20`. 313 entries; pair with Avedisova's "Catalogue of Star-Forming Regions" (VizieR `V/115`) for derived distances since the original catalog has none.
- **SIMBAD TAP query** for unified all-types fetch: object types `PN`, `RNe`, `HII`, `SNR` with a distance cut, via `astroquery.simbad`.

**Per-nebula data:** ID, common name, type, RA/Dec/distance → xyz, angular size → physical radius, illuminating star (for RNe).

**Rendering — this is where raymarching IS appropriate.** Each nebula occupies a small AABB (typically 1–50 pc), so per-nebula raymarching with 16–32 samples is dense in voxel-space and produces smooth volumetrics without the aliasing that killed the all-sky fog. See `memory/feedback_raymarch_for_nebulae.md` for context. Recover the fog shader pattern from git history at commit `5ad5444` if useful — it's a clip-space fullscreen triangle, ray-AABB slab intersection, fixed-step march with log-encoded density decode.

**Type-based visuals:**
- H II regions — warm reddish-pink, soft sphere with density falloff
- Planetary nebulae — greenish-teal, often shell-like; small central point + thin shell
- Reflection nebulae — bluish, irregular blob biased toward the illuminating star's colour (look up via ATHYG cross-ref)
- Supernova remnants — filamentary; pink→blue gradient by age

**No photographs.** All visuals procedural, matching the rest of the app's aesthetic.

**Watch out for:**
- Many PNe have only rough distance estimates; drop entries with no parsed distance.
- Filter to ATHYG-relevant volume — most distant PNe (>5 kpc) won't render usefully and clutter search.
- Procedural shape variation per type — without it, every nebula is a blob and the type encoding gets lost.

**Estimate:** 1–2 days for catalogs + ingest + rendering. The volumetric raymarching for the per-nebula effect is ~half the work.

title: Fullscreen mode + optional 'hide chrome' toggle
description:
Two related affordances filed in one issue because they both serve the same end (immersive / presentation / screenshot use):

1. Browser fullscreen — wire the Fullscreen API to a button in the brand box (or settings panel — decide during implementation) and a keyboard shortcut. Toggling enters/exits fullscreen on the canvas container so the panel and overlays are still visible inside fullscreen by default.

2. Hide chrome — a separate toggle that hides UI chrome (panel, top bar, overlays except the canvas itself + maybe scale bar) so the user can grab a clean shot or present without the UI. This works independently of fullscreen — you can hide chrome without going fullscreen, and vice versa.

Critical files:
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts — Esc progression at line 184 (must precede the existing observe→navigate fallback when fullscreen is active); add the new shortcut(s) here.
- src/client/help-modal.ts — must list both shortcuts.
- src/client/index.html, src/client/styles.css — chrome targets (panel container, brand box, top-bar, overlays). A single body[data-chrome-hidden] toggle is the cleanest selector hook; confirm during implementation that the existing layout containers cooperate.
- src/client/url-state.ts — natural home for state persistence (single bit each in the URL flag set). Skip persistence if it causes a confusing first-load (user lands on a page with no UI).
- src/client/brand-modal.ts (or controls.ts) — wherever the user-visible toggle button lands.

UX:
- Pressing Esc exits fullscreen first (if active), then falls through to the existing Esc progression in keyboard-shortcuts.ts:184.
- "Hide chrome" should be reversible by the same shortcut/control; show a small unobtrusive "show UI" affordance (corner button or bottom hint) so users aren't trapped.

Verify before close:
- Fullscreen toggle works in Chrome, Safari, Firefox.
- On exit, layout returns exactly as it was.
- Hide-chrome restores cleanly via shortcut and via the visible "show UI" affordance.
- Help modal lists both shortcuts.
- npm run typecheck and npm test pass.

Out of scope: any kiosk/auto-hide-on-idle behaviour, mobile-specific chrome rules (those land under the mobile epic).

title: 'F' shortcut: find a star and point camera at it (no warp)
description:
Add a keyboard shortcut F that opens the same search modal used by G, but instead of warping the camera to the chosen star, only orients the camera at it.

Behaviour by mode:
- Observe: rotate the camera (yaw + pitch) to point at the selected star. Animate the rotation rather than snap. FOV unchanged. Camera position unchanged.
- Navigate: orient the orbit gaze toward the star without travelling — i.e. rotate the trackball target so the star sits at the centre of the view, but do NOT warp the camera position to it (that's G's job) and do NOT mark it as focused (no focus-ring, no disc-mask). The animation should match observe-mode feel.

Critical files & reuses:
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts — existing G binding at line 89; add the F case adjacent. Modal-open + editable-target guards already in place around line 80.
- goModal (instantiated in keyboard-shortcuts.ts) — confirm during implementation that it can be parameterised by intent ("warp" vs "look") rather than duplicated.
- src/client/search.ts — the search/typeahead behind the modal.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — yaw/pitch source for the observe rotation animation.
- src/client/stellata.ts — trackball target / orbit gaze; the navigate-mode "swing target without travel" lives here.
- src/client/help-modal.ts — must be updated to list F.

UX:
- Pressing F while a modal is already open is a no-op (existing modal-open guard handles this).
- Esc cancels the search modal as today.

Verify before close:
- In observe, F → pick a star → camera smoothly rotates to it.
- In navigate, F → pick a star → orbit gaze swings to it; camera position unchanged; no focus indicators activate.
- npm run typecheck and npm test pass.
- Help modal shows F.

Out of scope: changing what G does. Adding F to the panel as a button (keyboard-only is fine for v1).

title: Phase 12b: Deep-space probes
description:
**Status:** Depends on Phase 12a's time-scrubber framework.

**Goal:** render the five interstellar probes — Pioneer 10, Pioneer 11, Voyager 1, Voyager 2, New Horizons — at their actual positions for the current `t`. Visible only when solar-system layer is active. A small but emotionally significant addition: "you can see how far they've gotten" against the scale of the solar system and the nearest stars.

### Probe data via NASA HORIZONS

| Probe        | Launch     | HORIZONS ID | Status                |
|--------------|------------|-------------|-----------------------|
| Pioneer 10   | 1972-03-03 | `-23`       | Silent since 2003-01-23 |
| Pioneer 11   | 1973-04-06 | `-24`       | Silent since 1995-09-30 |
| Voyager 2    | 1977-08-20 | `-32`       | Active                |
| Voyager 1    | 1977-09-05 | `-31`       | Active                |
| New Horizons | 2006-01-19 | `-98`       | Active                |

### Build script: `scripts/fetch-probe-trajectories.ts`

**Manual** — `npm run fetch:probes` only, not part of the default `build` chain. Same convention as the GCVS files (committed once, refreshed when upstream updates).

Per probe, query HORIZONS:
```
https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/api/horizons.api?format=json
  &COMMAND=<id>
  &CENTER=500@10
  &MAKE_EPHEM=YES
  &EPHEM_TYPE=VECTORS
  &VEC_TABLE=2
  &REF_PLANE=FRAME
  &START_TIME=<launch date>
  &STOP_TIME=2050-01-01
  &STEP_SIZE=30d
```

`REF_PLANE=FRAME` returns ICRS equatorial directly — same frame as ATHYG and the rest of the app. No ecliptic rotation needed.

Parse the `$$SOE…$$EOE` block, extract `JDTDB` and `X, Y, Z` (in AU) per step. Output `data/probes/{id}.json`:
```json
{
  "id": "voyager1",
  "label": "Voyager 1",
  "launchDate": "1977-09-05",
  "launchUnix": 242265600,
  "lastContactUnix": null,
  "trajectory": [
    { "jd": 2443413.5, "x": 0.12, "y": -0.03, "z": 0.01 },
    ...
  ]
}
```

`lastContactUnix` is null for active probes, populated for Pioneer 10 (`2003-01-23`) and Pioneer 11 (`1995-09-30`).

30-day step from launch to 2050: ~880 entries × ~50 bytes = ~45 KB per probe, ~225 KB total for 5 probes. Plain-text commit, no LFS.

### Sync to runtime

Mirror `data/probes/*.json` → `public/probes/*.json` via a small `scripts/sync-probes.ts` (same pattern as `sync-dust.ts`). Add to `build` and `dev` script chains. `public/probes/` gitignored.

### Runtime: `src/client/probe-loader.ts`

- Fetch all 5 JSONs in parallel (small enough — fire-and-forget at startup or lazy-load on first solar-system-layer activation).
- For a given `t`, find bracketing entries and **linearly interpolate** x/y/z. Probes move ~3–4 AU/year — 30-day samples are visually indistinguishable from the true smooth trajectory.
- Hide probe entirely if `t < launchUnix`.
- Visual flag for "extrapolated past last-contact": Pioneer 10 after 2003-01-23, Pioneer 11 after 1995-09-30. Render dimmer, with a "(signal lost)" label suffix.

### Probe rendering

Same instanced-quad pattern as planets. Tiny — a few px. Always-labelled when visible (probes are a discovery feature, the labels are the point). Optional: a thin fading trail showing the trajectory between launch and current `t`.

### Files

- New:
  - `scripts/fetch-probe-trajectories.ts` — manual HORIZONS fetcher.
  - `scripts/sync-probes.ts` — `data/probes/` → `public/probes/`.
  - `src/client/probe-loader.ts` — JSON load + interpolation.
  - `data/probes/{voyager1,voyager2,pioneer10,pioneer11,new-horizons}.json` — committed (no LFS).
- Touch:
  - `package.json` — `fetch:probes` script + `sync-probes` in chain.
  - `solar-system.ts` (from 12a) — register probe layer alongside planets, share label/render plumbing where sensible.
  - `.gitignore` — `/public/probes/`.
- After landing, extend the `CLAUDE.md` "Solar-system layer" section with the probe data pipeline and last-contact handling.

### Watch out for

- HORIZONS rate-limits and occasional schema drift. Fetch script should be defensive — clear error if `$$SOE` block is missing or columns are unexpected.
- Pre-launch hiding is per-probe based on `launchUnix`. Don't accidentally render a probe at `t = 0` (1970-01-01) where its trajectory array's first entry would extrapolate backward.
- Linear interpolation between widely-spaced 30-day samples is fine for visual purposes but should not be billed as "true ephemeris." Comment in code so future-you doesn't mistake it for a precision ephemeris API.
- Active probes' projected trajectories past *now* are HORIZONS forward-projections, not certainties. The data is correct as far as projections go but the wording in any tooltips should be careful.
- Annual-ish refresh: re-run `npm run fetch:probes`, commit the refreshed JSONs. Document the cadence in CLAUDE.md.

### Estimate

Half a day to a day. Fetch script is the bulk; runtime interpolation + rendering is small once 12a's planet rendering is in place.

### Deliberately excluded

- Other interstellar probes / spacecraft (Parker Solar Probe, JUICE, etc.) — not yet at solar-system-edge scale where they'd be visually meaningful in this layer.
- True ephemeris precision (sub-AU). Linear interpolation between monthly samples is good enough for visualisation.
- Trajectory-line rendering past current `t` for active probes. Trail is launch-to-now only; future projection isn't shown (would need clearer "this is a projection" framing in UI).

title: Phase 12a: Solar-system layer (time + planets + heliopause + scale rings)
description:
**Status:** Standalone. Introduces the wall-clock time scrubber that Phase 12b builds on.

**Goal:** when focused on Sol (or unfocused at Sol), render the local solar-system context: the 8 planets in their actual at-time positions, the heliopause boundary as an asymmetric shell, and faint reference rings at 1 AU and 50 AU. All driven by a new `t` time variable that defaults to the current wall-clock time and is user-scrubbable.

This is the first feature where the app cares about *actual* dates. Existing variable-star animation continues to use its independent cosmetic clock — `uTime` and the new `t` are orthogonal.

### The `t` time variable

Unix-seconds, double precision. Lives on the stellata filter state alongside other filter fields. Default is `Date.now() / 1000` evaluated at startup. URL state writes `t=<unix>` only when the user has scrubbed away from "now"; absence in URL = "live, defaults to current wall-clock at load time."

URL semantics intent: a shared link with `t=` preserves the sender's chosen date; a shared link without `t=` reads as "live", so it resolves to the receiver's local-now. This matches how shareable links should feel — a deliberately scrubbed pose freezes time, an idle pose stays live.

### Time-scrubber UI

Discreet. Lives in `.ui-bottom`. Broad idea: a short text readout showing the current `t` ("Live" when matching wall-clock within tolerance, otherwise a date), clickable to open an edit affordance. **Detailed widget choices deferred to implementation time** — the brief is "unobtrusive, doesn't dominate the bottom bar when nothing solar-system is visible."

Visible only when the solar-system layer is active (focused on Sol, or unfocused at Sol). Hidden otherwise.

### Sol-focus `minDistance` relaxation

When focused on Sol, drop `controls.minDistance` from `0.005 pc` (≈1031 AU) down to `~0.5 AU` ≈ `2.4e-6 pc`, so the user can fly into the inner solar system and resolve individual planets. Other focal stars retain the existing `0.005 pc` floor.

This is safe specifically for Sol because Sol sits at the world origin — the float32 jitter that bites at small distances *from non-origin focal stars* (CLAUDE.md "Camera near plane vs controls minDistance") doesn't apply when the focal frame is also the world frame.

`camera.near` already at `0.001 pc` is fine; for Sol-focus consider dropping to `1e-7 pc` (≈0.02 AU) so very close planet inspection isn't culled. Keep the strict-less-than invariant (`camera.near < minDistance`).

### Planet positions via VSOP87 (`astronomia` npm)

Add `astronomia` as a dependency. Provides full VSOP87D coefficient tables for all 8 planets with a clean JS API — no network calls, fully offline, ~500 KB added to bundle (acceptable; the data is the cost, not the algorithm).

Per-frame (when solar-system layer is visible):
1. `JDE = (t / 86400) + 2440587.5`
2. For each planet, call `astronomia.planetposition.Planet(...).position(JDE)` → `{ lon, lat, range }` heliocentric ecliptic, radians + AU.
3. Cartesian:
   ```
   x_ecl = R cos(B) cos(L)
   y_ecl = R cos(B) sin(L)
   z_ecl = R sin(B)
   ```
4. Rotate ecliptic → ICRS equatorial (ε = 23.4392911° at J2000):
   ```
   x_eq = x_ecl
   y_eq = y_ecl cos(ε) − z_ecl sin(ε)
   z_eq = y_ecl sin(ε) + z_ecl cos(ε)
   ```
5. Convert AU → parsecs (`/ 206264.806`) for the 3D scene.

Caching: VSOP87 evaluation is cheap but not free; cache results per-frame, recompute only when `t` changes.

### Planet rendering

Each planet as an instanced quad (similar to the star quads, separate `InstancedBufferGeometry`). Apparent-size with floor — physical AU sizes are tiny relative to orbital scale, so use `max(physicalRadius, minPx)` in the vertex shader. Body colour from a small per-planet constant table (Mercury grey, Mars rust, Jupiter banded yellow, etc.). Optional: a faint ring/label sphere texture at higher zoom.

Faint dashed orbit ellipses rendered as Three.js `Line` instances — one per planet, computed once from the planet's mean orbital elements (use VSOP87 at twelve evenly-spaced mean anomalies and connect, or use Keplerian elements directly). Re-renders only on UI-toggle, not per frame.

DOM labels for each planet — extend the existing `meta` overlay pattern or add a small `solar-system-labels.ts` module that does project-and-place per frame.

### Heliopause — asymmetric ellipsoid

Best-known shape per IBEX observations + Voyager 1/2 crossing data:
- **Apex (upwind, toward solar apex):** ~122 AU. Voyager 1 crossed at ~121 AU on 2012-08-25.
- **Flanks (perpendicular to apex direction):** ~115 AU. Voyager 2 crossed at ~119 AU on 2018-11-05 at ~67° from apex.
- **Heliotail (downwind):** ~200 AU. Recent IBEX/Cassini ENA data + global heliospheric models suggest 200–350 AU; the tail is the least-constrained direction. 200 AU is a defensible mid-estimate.

Apex direction: solar apex of motion through the local interstellar medium. ICRS approx **RA 17h53m, Dec +27.4°** (Frisch & Slavin 2013 LISM inflow direction). Document the source in code so future sessions don't ad-hoc this.

**Implementation as a clean ellipsoid + offset:**
- Total length along apex–tail axis = 122 + 200 = 322 AU.
- Major semi-axis (along apex–tail) = 161 AU.
- Equatorial semi-axes (in flank plane) = 115 AU.
- Centre of ellipsoid offset from Sol by (200 − 122)/2 = 39 AU toward the heliotail (anti-apex). Result: upwind boundary lands at 122 AU from Sol, tail boundary at 200 AU.

Construction: `SphereGeometry(1, 64, 32)` → non-uniform scale `(115, 115, 161)` along its local axes → orient so local +Z is the anti-apex direction → translate by 39 AU along that direction. Convert AU → pc for the scene.

Material: low-opacity additive shell (transparent, `depthWrite=false`), or a wireframe mesh. Pick one and tune. Label "Heliopause (~120 AU upwind)" — the asymmetry is intentional and the label should acknowledge it without over-specifying the tail.

Static — no `t` dependence (heliopause shape doesn't measurably change on human timescales).

### Scale rings — 1 AU and 50 AU

Faint `LineLoop` circles in the ICRS xy-plane (close enough to the ecliptic for visual reference; not the ecliptic plane proper, which is tilted 23.4°). Scale anchors:
- **1 AU** — Earth's orbit, inner-system context.
- **50 AU** — Kuiper Belt outer edge, outer-system context.

Visible only when solar-system layer is active. Optional toggle in the panel (default on).

### Files

- New:
  - `src/client/solar-system.ts` — VSOP87 driver, planet rendering, label placement, ring meshes.
  - `src/client/heliopause.ts` — static asymmetric mesh.
  - `src/client/time-scrubber.ts` — discreet UI in `.ui-bottom`.
- Touch:
  - `stellata.ts` — filter-state `t` field, Sol-focus `minDistance` swap, solar-system layer hookup.
  - `controls.ts` — possibly a panel toggle for orbits/rings.
  - `url-state.ts` — `t=` parameter (omit when "live").
  - `index.html` / `styles.css` — time-scrubber container in `.ui-bottom`, ring/orbit visibility classes.
  - `package.json` — add `astronomia` dependency.
- After landing, update `CLAUDE.md`:
  - Add a "Solar-system layer" section (VSOP87 coordinate transform chain, Sol-focus minDistance carve-out, t vs uTime distinction, heliopause apex direction reference).
  - Note in "Things deliberately kept out": time-series proper motion of stars is *still* out of scope — `t` only affects solar-system bodies.

### Watch out for

- **Sol-focus `minDistance` is a per-focus override.** When focus switches from Sol to another star, the floor must snap back to `0.005 pc` *before* the new focus's recenter pulls the camera in. `setFocus` is the right hook.
- **`t` vs `uTime`.** Don't accidentally couple them. `uTime` keeps ticking at `uSecondsPerDay = 0.2` regardless of where the scrubber is. Variable-star pulsation is cosmetic; mixing in wall-clock would either freeze pulsation when scrubbed or produce nonsensical strobing when the scrubber moves fast.
- **Ecliptic ↔ equatorial obliquity.** Use J2000 ε = 23.4392911° consistently. Don't reach for the time-varying obliquity term unless someone proves it makes a visible difference (it doesn't on visualisation timescales).
- **Solar-system layer is only meaningful from Sol-focus.** If you render planets from a Tau Ceti view they'd be sub-pixel close to Sol anyway, but explicit gating avoids edge cases (e.g. orbit ellipse meshes that shouldn't render at all when not at Sol).
- **`camera.near` invariant.** Anything that drops `minDistance` must keep `camera.near < minDistance`. Trivial but easy to miss when both shift together for Sol-focus.

### Estimate

1.5–2 days. VSOP87 wiring is mechanical (the library does the heavy lifting), heliopause mesh + scale rings are quick, time-scrubber UI is the most variable bit. Most time goes to making planet rendering look good across the new much-wider zoom range.

### Deliberately excluded

- Moons (Earth's Moon, Galilean satellites, Titan, etc.). VSOP87 doesn't cover these and the data plumbing for satellite ephemerides is a separate effort.
- Asteroids and minor planets. Out of scope for v1.
- Time-series proper motion of stars. Stars remain snapshot-only; `t` is solar-system-only.
- Time-of-day / Earth rotation effects. The scene is heliocentric; Earth's daily rotation has no meaning here.
- Real-time mode that animates `t` automatically forward. The scrubber is for explicit positioning; "Live" mode resolves to the current wall-clock at frame time but doesn't sweep.

title: Phase 11: Exoplanets around host stars
Phase 10: Wander mode (autonomous gravity-field camera)
description:
**Status:** Standalone exploration feature. No dependencies on other phases — can ship any time the catalog format is stable.

**Goal:** the camera behaves as a particle moving through a precomputed gravitational field derived from the star catalog. Hit `G`, the camera drifts autonomously through the local stellar neighbourhood, following gradients of stellar mass density. Click / `Esc` / `G` again to exit, leaving the camera wherever it ended up. Naturalistic exploration without steering — dense clusters pull the camera in, ridges deflect it.

Two parts: (1) an offline precompute script that builds a `gravity.bin` asset, (2) runtime integration in `stellata.ts` following the warp pattern.

### Part 1: `scripts/build-gravity.ts`

**Bounding volume.** Read star positions from `catalog.bin` (inline a small reader, same approach `verify-catalog.ts` uses — no need to factor a shared module). Compute max distance from Sol across all stars, call this `R`. Grid is a cube of side `2R` centred at the origin, voxel side ≈ `2R / 128`. Voxel centres outside the bounding sphere of radius `R` store a zero gradient — no extrapolation past the catalog.

**Grid.** 128³ voxels → ~25 MB output (3 × float32 per voxel). Coarse relative to inter-star spacing near Sol (~1 pc), but that's desirable — wander should feel like ridges and basins of density, not orbital snap-ins on individual stars.

**Mass proxy.**
```
luminosity = 10 ^ ((4.74 − absmag) / 2.5)
mass       = max(0, luminosity ^ MASS_EXPONENT)
```
Default `MASS_EXPONENT = 0.5`. The physical mass-luminosity relation gives ~0.286, but 0.5 keeps dim stars from being completely swamped — a tuning lever, not a physics value. Document the physical reference in the comment.

**Gradient computation.**
```
for each voxel center p:
  for each star i:
    r_vec   = star_position − p
    dist_sq = dot(r_vec, r_vec) + SOFTENING²
    g      += mass_i × r_vec / (dist_sq × sqrt(dist_sq))
```
Vectors are NOT normalised — magnitude encodes field strength.

**Performance.** 128³ × 313k ≈ 660 G ops. Run in Node `worker_threads`, chunked one Z-slice per task, target <5 min on a modern laptop. Optional optimisation: per-voxel cull stars beyond e.g. 500 pc (at softening 2 pc, a star at 500 pc contributes ~1/250000 the weight of one at 1 pc). Worth measuring before adding — could shave an order of magnitude.

**Output `public/gravity.bin`.**
```
Header (32 bytes):
  0–3    ASCII "GRV1"
  4–7    uint32 grid dimension N (128)
  8–11   float32 grid half-extent in parsecs (= R)
  12–15  float32 softening value used (informational)
  16–19  float32 mass exponent used (informational)
  20–31  reserved (zeros)

Body:
  N³ records × 12 bytes — float32 x, y, z gradient
  Index order: x + y*N + z*N*N (x varies fastest)
```

Add to `.gitignore` alongside `catalog.bin`. Add `build:gravity` to `package.json`, chained after `build:catalog` in both `dev` and `build` scripts (depends on `catalog.bin` existing). Use the same `isUpToDate` pattern as `build-catalog.ts`, gated on `catalog.bin` mtime + the script's own mtime + the build-baked constants.

### Part 2: `src/client/gravity-loader.ts`

Following `catalog-loader.ts` structure:
- Fetch `gravity.bin`, validate magic + version.
- Expose `gridN`, `halfExtent`, `softening`, `massExponent`, `gradients: Float32Array` (length `N³ × 3`).
- Export `sampleGradient(absolutePos: THREE.Vector3): THREE.Vector3` with trilinear interpolation. Return zero for positions outside the bounding sphere.

Load in parallel with `catalog.bin` and `search-index.json` in `main.ts`.

### Part 3: Wander state in `stellata.ts`

**State machine.** Add `'wander'` mode alongside warp. Same disable pattern: `controls.enabled = false`, `body.classList.toggle('wandering', true)`, `pointerup` short-circuited, URL writer skips camera-hash updates while active.

Entry: keyboard shortcut `G` (verified free; `W` is warp; `Esc` / `Space` skip warp). Exit: any `pointerdown`, `Esc`, or `G` again.

**Focus interaction (option (a)).** Activating wander **unfocuses** any current focal star (calls `setFocus(null)`), which also recentres the floating origin to Sol. The wander particle then operates in a clean absolute frame for the duration of the mode.

**Coordinate-frame correctness — CRITICAL.** The gradient field is in absolute Sol-centred coordinates, matching `catalog.positions`. After unfocus on entry, `worldOffset = (0, 0, 0)` and `camera.position` is in absolute space, so initial sampling is direct. But once the wander loop calls `recenterOrigin(...)` for precision (see below), `worldOffset` becomes non-zero and `camera.position` shifts toward zero. From that point all gradient samples must be at `camera.position + worldOffset`, never at `camera.position` alone. Same for the lookahead and any read-back. This is the single most common way for the field to silently drift — call it out in code comments around the sampling.

**Particle state.**
```typescript
private _wanderActive = false
private _wanderVelocity = new THREE.Vector3()
private _wanderDistanceSinceRecenter = 0
```

**Per-frame update `updateWander(dt)`.**
1. `samplePos = camera.position + worldOffset`. If `velocity.lengthSq() > ε²`, `samplePos += normalize(velocity) × WANDER_LOOKAHEAD_PC`.
2. `accel = sampleGradient(samplePos) × WANDER_GRAVITY_SCALE`.
3. `velocity += accel × dt × WANDER_SIM_TIME_SCALE`.
4. `velocity *= pow(WANDER_VELOCITY_DAMPING, dt × 60)` — anchors the "0.99 per frame" lever to 60 fps so feel is frame-rate independent.
5. Clamp `|velocity| ≤ WANDER_MAX_SPEED_PC_PER_S`.
6. `step = velocity × dt × WANDER_SIM_TIME_SCALE`. `camera.position += step`. `controls.target += step` (keep target moving with camera so orbit orientation is preserved on exit).
7. `_wanderDistanceSinceRecenter += step.length()`. If it crosses ~1 pc, call `recenterOrigin(camera.position + worldOffset)` and reset the counter. Keeps float32 precision good when wandering far from Sol.

**Initial kick (entry).** Field-scaled with a floor — guarantees motion even in a void, and scales naturally with `GRAVITY_SCALE` without a separate retune.
```
g         = sampleGradient(camera.position + worldOffset)
v0        = max(WANDER_KICK_FLOOR_PC_PER_S,
                WANDER_KICK_TIME_S × |g| × WANDER_GRAVITY_SCALE)
direction = normalize(controls.target − camera.position)
            (fallback: +Z if degenerate, never normalise zero)
_wanderVelocity = direction × v0
```

**Tuning levers.** Define as named constants at the top of the wander section. Comments must call out runtime vs build-baked:
- `WANDER_SOFTENING_PC = 2.0` — **build-baked**; must match `build-gravity.ts`. Changing requires regenerating `gravity.bin`.
- `WANDER_MASS_EXPONENT = 0.5` — **build-baked**; same constraint.
- `WANDER_GRAVITY_SCALE = 0.1` — runtime.
- `WANDER_VELOCITY_DAMPING = 0.99` — runtime; interpreted as per-frame at 60 fps, applied as `pow(damping, dt × 60)`.
- `WANDER_MAX_SPEED_PC_PER_S = 20.0` — runtime.
- `WANDER_SIM_TIME_SCALE = 5.0` — runtime.
- `WANDER_LOOKAHEAD_PC = 2.0` — runtime.
- `WANDER_KICK_TIME_S = 2.0` — runtime; "free-fall seconds" worth of initial kick velocity.
- `WANDER_KICK_FLOOR_PC_PER_S = 1.0` — runtime; absolute floor so voids still produce motion.

### Files

- New: `scripts/build-gravity.ts`, `src/client/gravity-loader.ts`.
- Touch: `stellata.ts` (mode + state + `updateWander` + key handler), `main.ts` (parallel load, pass loader to `Stellata`), `package.json` (script + chain), `.gitignore` (`/public/gravity.bin`), `styles.css` (optional `body.wandering` style — e.g. cursor change or subtle vignette to signal "you are not driving").
- After landing, update `CLAUDE.md`: extend the floating-origin section with the "sample in absolute frame" contract and add a new "Wander mode" section.

### Watch out for

- Floating-origin sampling — covered above; single biggest landmine.
- Trilinear interpolation must clamp grid indices: a fractional voxel near the cube edge can land at `N − 1 + ε`; `floor` gives `N − 1` but `floor + 1` would read past the end.
- Unfocus-on-entry is intentional (option (a)). Don't be tempted to "preserve focus for the panel" — focus drives `worldOffset` and decoupling them is messy.
- Kick direction normalises `target − position`; defensively guard the degenerate case (target == position) with a fixed fallback vector so we never normalise zero.
- URL-sync `onFrame` skip-while-warping needs to extend to wander — we don't want intermediate poses serialised.
- 25 MB `gravity.bin` is a meaningful download; serve gzipped (Vite default for `public/` handles this) and load eagerly initially. Revisit lazy-load only if startup latency complaints surface.

### Estimate

1–1.5 days. Most of it is build-script worker setup plus tuning the nine levers to a feel that's neither stuck-on-Sol nor slingshot-out-of-the-catalog. The `stellata.ts` integration is small and follows the warp pattern.

### Deliberately excluded

- UI controls for the tuning levers — constants in source only.
- A wander button (keyboard shortcut only).
- Apparent-magnitude or visibility input to steering — field is mass × position only.
- Any changes to dust / nebula rendering.

title: Phase 2: Revisit dust visualisation layer
description:
**Status:** infrastructure complete, default off, needs render tuning.

**The fundamental tension:** real interstellar dust is *dark*. It absorbs starlight; it doesn't emit. The current particle implementation renders dust as additive warm-brown glows, which is visually pretty but inverts physical reality. A user looking at the additive fog sees "glowing dust everywhere" when in reality those regions are *darker* than empty space.

The per-star extinction layer (Phase 1, shipped) is the physically honest representation — stars behind dust dim and redden. That's what an observer at Sol would actually see.

**Open questions to settle before re-enabling particles:**

1. **What's the role of the visualisation layer?** Two possible framings:
   - **(a)** "Show me where the dust is" — a non-physical overlay, clearly marked as visualisation, similar to how astronomical maps overlay contour lines. Additive glow is fine here, just label it as such.
   - **(b)** "Make the dust visible against starlight" — physically motivated darkening using `MultiplyBlending` so dust patches occlude stars rather than emitting light. This is closer to truth but only visible where there are bright background stars.
2. **Particle splat tuning** — current values (`MIN_PX = 30`, `MAX_PX = 80`, brightness range 0.04–0.15) are a starting point; needs side-by-side testing to find the splat-size / brightness combination where dense regions feel like fog and diffuse regions stay below the perceptual floor. The goal is "smooth from overlap, never individual specks."
3. **Importance sampling tradeoff** — current density-weighted sampling over-represents dense cores; sqrt or log weighting gives more diffuse-region representation but flattens the cores. A two-tier approach (80% density-weighted + 20% uniform-where-d>threshold) might be the right balance.
4. **UX entry point** — currently console-only via `stellata.setParticleStrength(x)`. Promoting to a user-facing toggle needs UI design (probably a checkbox under "Display Settings" with a short label like "Show interstellar dust").

**Files to touch:**
- `src/client/shaders/dust-particle.vert.glsl` — splat sizing, brightness
- `src/client/shaders/dust-particle.frag.glsl` — falloff curve, blend mode
- `scripts/build-dust.py` `sample_particles()` — importance-sampling
- `src/client/stellata.ts` `attachDustParticles()` — blend mode if changing

**What NOT to change:** the per-star extinction layer is shipped and working. Don't tune it during particle work; it's the physical baseline.

**Reference for the abandoned fullscreen-fog approach:** see `memory/feedback_raymarch_for_nebulae.md` — fullscreen raymarch produced unfixable banding/jitter at far zoom. Don't go back to it for the all-sky volume; it's the right tool only for small per-nebula AABBs.

value: Frozen external data (no live fetch at build time). For any external scientific catalog used in Stellata's pipeline (stellar, ISM, nebular, exoplanetary, etc.), build scripts must read from a file committed under data/ (LFS-tracked when large). The build must be fully self-contained — no live network fetch as part of npm run build or the Python preprocessor. Why: Alex wants the build to keep working long-term even when external sources go offline, change schemas, or move URLs. Existing pattern (athyg_33_classic_ids.csv, gcvs5.txt, crossid.txt, stellarium-modern-skyculture.json, Edenhofer dust via committed data/dust/*.bin) reflects this — refresh from upstream is an explicit, manual, infrequent step, not a build dependency. How to apply: new external data — fetch once (manually or via one-shot helper), commit raw file under data/ (LFS if >1 MB), document source URL + retrieval date in CLAUDE.md/SCIENCE.md. Build scripts read from data/<file>, never via requests.get / urllib / astroquery against live endpoints. If you write a fetch helper, name it explicitly (e.g. scripts/refresh-clouds.py) and gate it from npm run build. Applies to JSON/CSV/FITS/HDF5/TSV catalogs, sky-culture JSON, dust map binaries — anything sourced from outside the repo.

Use raymarch for nebulae, not for the all-sky dust volume. When implementing nebulae, volumetric raymarching is appropriate — each nebula occupies a small AABB (typically <=10–50 pc) so a 16–32 sample raymarch is dense in voxel-space, producing smooth volumetrics without aliasing/jitter. Why: a fullscreen raymarch of the entire 2500 pc dust cube was tried for a visible-fog Phase 2; with 24–32 samples per pixel, the step size at far zoom (camera 2.5+ kpc out) was ~80 pc — much larger than the 5 pc voxel size — producing axis-aligned tile artefacts that even per-pixel jitter, half-res rendering, and temporal smoothing couldn't fix without making the fog disappear. Verdict: extinction already conveys dust beautifully; the volumetric pass was overhead for diminishing returns. How to apply: for each nebula, render a small raymarched volume bounded by its catalog AABB. Shader pattern is the one in the now-deleted dust-fog.frag.glsl (recover from git history at commit 5ad5444 if needed) — clip-space fullscreen triangle, ray-AABB slab intersection, fixed-step march with log-encoded density decode, scoped to one nebula at a time and only when the camera is near it. Procedural noise/turbulence per-nebula type would make them feel less uniform.

Defer doc updates until the final commit. Don't edit CLAUDE.md, README.md, anything under docs/, or SCIENCE.md while implementing a feature in Stellata — treat code changes as the only deliverable until commit time. When the user asks to commit and push, audit all four sources against the actual final diff and update only what the change really invalidates. Why: mid-session doc edits become churn — direction shifts, features get dropped, parameters rename, knob values move; the paragraph written early ends up describing something that no longer exists. How to apply: during implementation confine edits to code, shaders, tests; if a code comment references a doc section about to be wrong, leave it and surface at commit time. At commit time, grep the final diff for renames, removed knobs, new uniforms, behavioural shifts, anything user-visible — open CLAUDE.md, README.md, docs/, SCIENCE.md and update only what's now stale. Skip ones that don't need changes.

GCVS data source. GCVS (General Catalogue of Variable Stars) data for Stellata was downloaded from the Sternberg Astronomical Institute: http://www.sai.msu.su/gcvs/gcvs/index.htm — this site carries the authoritative GCVS maintained by Samus et al. Files in repo from this source: data/gcvs5.txt (main variable-star catalogue, fixed-width text, pipe-separated fields including period, amplitude, type, one cross-reference catalog ID per star) and data/crossid.txt (cross-identification file mapping GCVS designations to HD/HIP/Tycho/SAO/other catalog IDs, used to broaden the AT-HYG cross-match). When refreshing GCVS data in the future, return to the same URL. Licence: free for research/educational use with attribution.

Rectangular block artifact in star render — diagnosed (deferred to Phase 7). Investigation 2026-05-01: rectangular dense-star patches visible at high maxAppMag are NOT a renderer bug, binary-inference artefact, or float clustering. They're AT-HYG cross-match completeness boundaries — specifically Tycho-2 rows whose Gaia DR3 distance lookup succeeded vs those where it didn't. AT-HYG only emits x0/y0/z0 for successful matches, and scripts/build-catalog.ts drops the rest in readStars (the noCoords filter). Gaia DR3 success rate is spatially non-uniform along scanning-law caustics + ecliptic footprint, so the surviving population shows axis-aligned rectangular density variations. Visibility threshold ~mag 9; the Tycho-sourced population (mean mag ~10.2, complete to V_T ~11.5) is filtered out at naked-eye 6.5 and mostly out at binoculars 10.5; squares only become obvious in the 'all' preset at mag 15. Why pinned: Phase 7 (mag-12 ingest) will likely amplify the artefact since denser ingest from same AT-HYG pipeline inherits the same Tycho+Gaia-DR3 completeness map. Real fixes: (a) wait for Gaia DR4 / fresher AT-HYG, or (b) explicitly filter rendered stars by dist_src and/or apparent mag. How to apply: don't re-investigate as renderer bug. Canonical write-up lives in SCIENCE.md §Stellar catalog ingestion. Relevant CSV columns when Phase 7 forces the call: pos_src (~99% T), dist_src (~98% G_R3), and mag.

Don't loop on failed web fetches. If a URL access fails (404, blocked, auth wall, AI-scraper filter, etc.), do NOT keep trying alternative transports (WebFetch -> curl -> different URL -> etc.) in a loop. After at most 2 attempts, stop and ask the user to fetch the content themselves and paste it in, drop the file into the repo, or give the information directly. Why: the user flagged this explicitly — they don't want time and tokens burned on transport attempts that aren't converging. It's faster for them to grab it manually than to thrash. How to apply: on the first failure, try one alternative. On the second failure, ask. Tell them the exact URL being attempted so they can fetch it themselves with minimal context-switching.

Prefer small topical commits: when a change touches multiple concerns, split them into separate commits each with a focused subject line and brief why-body. Why: validated previously — splitting Git LFS setup from a floating-origin jitter fix into two commits was accepted without pushback. One-concept-per-commit keeps the repo's story legible and makes revert/bisect surgical. How to apply: when there are unrelated staged changes, don't reach for one omnibus commit message — stage and commit each concern separately. Err toward more commits, not fewer. Keep using HEREDOC commit format with the Co-Authored-By: Claude trailer.

Game concept built on Stellata, deferred. At the start of the 2026-04-23/24 session, Alex said 'I had a fun idea for a game based on Starfield...' and explicitly deferred it in favour of rendering improvements. The idea itself wasn't described. Why: rendering work had visible results he wanted first; the game is a bigger scope he's saving. How to apply: next time he resumes Stellata without a specific ask, it's fair to proactively prompt: 'do you want to pick up the game idea now, or is there another rendering/UX item first?' Don't start designing the game unprompted — he has a specific concept in mind that hasn't been shared.

Stellata main branch is PR-protected. The main branch on the Stellata repo requires a pull request for any code change — direct pushes to main are not allowed. Why: user told me on 2026-05-01 that PRs are required to merge into main. (No CI/branch-protection details were given, just the policy.) How to apply: when the user asks to commit and push a change, branch off main first (e.g. git switch -c <topic>), commit there, git push -u origin <topic>, then open a PR with gh pr create. Don't git push origin main directly.

Milky Way visual benchmark image. Reference image: https://cdn.eso.org/images/screen/eso0932a.jpg (ESO eso0932a panorama). Alex uses this as a visual ground-truth benchmark when evaluating Stellata's physical model — primarily the volumetric Milky Way disc + bulge (docs/milky-way.md) and how the integrated star field reads against the real sky. When Alex asks about Milky Way appearance, brightness calibration, dust banding, or whether something 'looks right', consider comparing to this image.

Famous stars missing from rendered catalogue. AT-HYG has several famous stars with empty 3D position fields and the build-catalog script drops any row with empty x0/y0/z0/plx columns without warning. Known examples: HIP 5165 (alpha Phe / Ankaa) and HIP 89341 (mu Sgr / Polis) are in KNOWN_MISSING_HIPS in scripts/build-catalog.ts; HIP 107259 (mu Cep / Herschel's Garnet Star) is NOT in that list and is silently dropped. Why: KNOWN_MISSING_HIPS is only consulted when a Stellarium constellation-line endpoint can't be resolved; arbitrary missing-search-stars don't surface there, so a star can be missing from render and the build still succeeds cleanly. How to apply: when investigating missing stars, (1) grep HIP_NUMBER in data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv — empty x/y/z cells = AT-HYG lacks parallax. (2) Options to rescue a specific star: patch a synthetic row during build from a hand-measured parallax (Gaia DR3, Hipparcos re-reduction), or switch data sources for that subset. (3) Don't conflate 'missing from render' with 'missing from search' — the same CSV feeds both.

Alex on the Stellata project. Stellata (renamed from 'Starfield' in May 2026) is Alex's personal side project, not a work project — he's the sole contributor. Domain: stellata.xyz (Alex owns it). The GitHub repo and local working directory are still named 'starfield' at time of writing — Alex plans to rename both manually as a follow-up. Old URL alxm.me/starfield/ is being retired; new deploy path alxm.me/stellata/ (pending future migration to stellata.xyz proper). Technical depth: comfortable reasoning about graphics/rendering at a low level — float32 precision, catastrophic cancellation, instanced quad rendering, shader math, matrix composition. Discuss emulated-double-precision, floating-origin, GPU-vs-CPU tradeoffs directly without scaffolding. Collaboration style: values elegance explicitly ('come up with the most elegant solution'), not just correctness. Prefers to probe a hypothesis empirically before committing to an implementation — will ask what stars to test, what URLs to hit, etc. Treat 'exploratory' as literal, not a hedge. Authorises destructive git ops (force-push to main) readily when reasoning is sound and he's the only contributor; still check before doing it.
